title: Efficient generation of epitope-targeted antibodies with Germinal - Nature Biotechnology
text:
AbstractObtaining antibodies to specific protein targets is a widely important yet experimentally laborious process. Meanwhile, computational methods for antibody design have been limited by low success rates that require resource-intensive screening. Here we introduce Germinal, a broadly enabling generative pipeline that designs antibodies against specific epitopes with nanomolar binding affinities while requiring only low-n experimental testing. Our method co-optimizes antibody structure and sequence by integrating a structure predictor with an antibody-specific protein language model to perform de novo design of functional complementarity-determining regions onto a user-specified structural framework. When tested against four diverse protein targets, Germinal designed functional antibodies across all targets and binder formats, testing only 43–101 designs for each antigen. Validated designs also exhibited robust expression in mammalian cells and high sequence and structural novelty. We provide open-source code and full computational and experimental protocols to facilitate wide adoption.

Fig. 1: Efficient antibody generation through joint optimization of AF-M and IgLM with Germinal.Fig. 2: Germinal’s design stage steers generations toward natural antibody-like properties through structural and sequence guidance.Fig. 3: Biophysical characterization of Germinal-designed binders.Fig. 4: Cryo-EM and mutational mapping of epitopes targeted by Germinal-designed binders.

Data availability

The cryo-EM map and atomic model of the PDL1 scFv H5 complex were deposited to the EM Data Bank under accession code EMD-77181 and the PDB under accession code 35TL, respectively. We also uploaded raw data to Zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20221721)90. All data, including the sequences of all tested designs and template plasmid maps used in this study, are available in the main text or its Supplementary Information.
Code availability

Code for running the pipeline is available from GitHub (https://github.com/SantiagoMille/germinal).
